craft_guidance: 1. IDENTIFY ENTRY POINT: URL parameters (url=, redirect=, callback=, webhook=), file imports, PDF generators, image fetchers, API integrations.
2. TEST CONNECTIVITY: Send to Collaborator first. If callback received, SSRF confirmed. If not, try blind via timing (slow internal vs fast external).
3. BYPASS FILTERS: IP blocked? Use decimal (2130706433), octal (0177.0.0.1), hex (0x7f000001), IPv6 ([::1]), DNS rebinding, or redirect from your server.
4. CLOUD METADATA: AWS=169.254.169.254 (IMDSv2 needs token PUT first), GCP=metadata.google.internal, Azure=169.254.169.254 (needs Metadata:true header).
5. PROTOCOL PIVOTING: http:// blocked? Try file:// (local read), gopher:// (Redis/SMTP), dict:// (port scan), ftp://, ldap://.
6. ESCALATION: Metadata creds -> AWS CLI -> S3/RDS access. Redis -> write web shell or SSH key. Elasticsearch -> dump indices. Internal admin panels -> further exploitation.
7. BLIND DETECTION: No response body? Use DNS (Collaborator), timing (internal=slow, external=fast), or error differences (connection refused vs timeout vs success).
8. REDIRECT CHAIN: Target fetches your server -> 302 redirect to internal IP. Bypasses hostname-based filters that only check initial URL.

internal.description: SSRF probes targeting internal network resources and localhost services

probes.description: SSRF to localhost root — length diff indicates internal content served
SSRF to localhost SSH port — SSH banner in response confirms internal reach
SSRF to localhost Redis — Redis error banner confirms internal reach
SSRF via 0.0.0.0 alias for localhost bypass
Out-of-band SSRF detection via collaborator callback URL

localhost_bypass.description: SSRF localhost bypass probes — alternative IP representations that resolve to 127.0.0.1 but evade string-based blocklists

probes.description: Shortened localhost IP — 127.1 resolves to 127.0.0.1 on most systems, bypasses full-quad filters
Octal notation for 127.0.0.1 — 0177 = 127 in octal; many URL parsers resolve this to localhost
Hex notation for 127.0.0.1 — 0x7f = 127 in hex; bypasses decimal IP blocklists
Decimal integer notation for 127.0.0.1 — 2130706433 = 0x7F000001; evades dotted-quad filters entirely
IPv6 loopback address — canonical localhost in IPv6, bypasses IPv4-only blocklists
Zero IP — resolves to 0.0.0.0 which maps to localhost on many systems

cloud_metadata.description: SSRF probes targeting cloud provider metadata endpoints (AWS, GCP, Azure, DigitalOcean, Oracle, Alibaba)

probes.description: AWS IMDSv1 metadata endpoint — ami-id or instance-id confirms SSRF to AWS metadata
AWS IAM credentials endpoint — role name in response reveals attached IAM role
GCP metadata endpoint — requires Metadata-Flavor header bypass
Azure IMDS endpoint — compute details confirm Azure SSRF
DigitalOcean metadata endpoint — droplet_id or region in response confirms SSRF to DO metadata
Oracle Cloud IMDS endpoint — compartmentId or shape in response confirms SSRF to OCI metadata
Alibaba Cloud ECS metadata endpoint — instance-id or region-id confirms SSRF to Alibaba Cloud metadata

ipv6_bypass.description: SSRF bypass using IPv6 representations of localhost — evades blocklists checking for 127.0.0.1 in string form

probes.description: IPv4-mapped IPv6 address for localhost — bypasses simple string match on 127.0.0.1
Full-form IPv4-mapped IPv6 localhost — alternative notation evading short-form filters
IPv6 loopback address (::1) — canonical localhost in IPv6, bypasses IPv4-only blocklists

url_bypass.description: SSRF filter bypass via URL parsing ambiguities — at-sign, fragment, shortened IP, zero IP, backslash tricks from HackTricks

probes.description: At-sign URL parsing trick — some parsers use evil.com as host while fetchers use 127.0.0.1 as auth host
Fragment bypass — some validators stop at # treating 127.0.0.1 as fragment, fetcher uses it as host
Shortened localhost IP — 127.1 resolves to 127.0.0.1 on most systems, bypasses full-quad filters
Zero IP — resolves to 0.0.0.0 which maps to localhost on many systems
Backslash before @ trick — some URL parsers treat backslash as path separator, others as authority delimiter

protocol_variant.description: SSRF via alternative protocol schemes — tests whether server-side fetchers support non-HTTP protocols that can interact with internal services

probes.description: FTP protocol SSRF — FTP banner (220, vsFTPd, ProFTPD) in response confirms server-side FTP client support
Gopher to Redis SSRF — gopher protocol sends raw bytes to Redis; redis_version in response confirms gopher support and Redis access
Gopher to Memcached SSRF — sends stats command via gopher; STAT in response confirms Memcached access
LDAP protocol SSRF — LDAP error or connection message confirms server-side LDAP client support and internal reach
Dict protocol to Redis SSRF — dict:// sends raw command to Redis; redis_version confirms dict support and Redis access
File protocol SSRF — reads local files; non-empty response with short content (hostname) confirms file:// scheme is allowed

dns_rebinding.description: SSRF via DNS rebinding — exploits time-of-check vs time-of-use when server validates DNS then fetches, allowing second resolution to point to internal IP

probes.description: DNS rebinding baseline — send Collaborator URL to confirm server-side fetch occurs; Collaborator DNS interaction is prerequisite for rebinding attack
Hex IP subdomain rebinding probe — some DNS resolvers resolve hex-encoded IPs in subdomains to 127.0.0.1; Collaborator interaction + internal content confirms rebinding

redirect_based.description: SSRF via open redirect chains — server fetches a URL that redirects to an internal resource

probes.description: SSRF via open redirect — server-side fetch follows redirect to cloud metadata endpoint; check collaborator for interaction
AWS EC2 hostname metadata — short string response is high-confidence SSRF confirmation

protocol_smuggling.description: Non-HTTP URI schemes that some HTTP clients still resolve. gopher:// is the king for raw-byte cross-protocol attacks (Redis, memcached, MySQL, SMTP). Java URL.openConnection accepts ftp://, file://, jar://, netdoc:// historically. PHP allows phar://, expect://, glob://. Curl's -L follows file:// across redirects.

probes.description: gopher:// to Redis 6379 — sends raw RESP-protocol bytes via HTTP-style fetch. flushall is destructive; replace with INFO for safety. Confirm by issuing a benign command first (Rule 5).
gopher:// to memcached 11211 — STATS command. Output dumps server stats and key prefixes.
dict:// scheme to memcached. Curl-style libs accept dict://. Less stealthy than gopher but no encoding required.
file:// scheme — Java URL.openConnection, PHP file_get_contents, Python urllib accept this. /etc/passwd content confirms file read.
jar: scheme on Java — file:// inside a jar URI. Some Java HTTP clients still resolve.
netdoc: legacy Java scheme that resolves like file://. Many WAFs miss it.
PHP phar:// triggers unserialize on .phar metadata — distinct attack path from raw read. Useful when file_get_contents is filtered for file://.

dns_rebinding_advanced.description: DNS rebinding 2024+ variants. Classic A-record TTL-0 swap is well-known; new vectors abuse SVCB/HTTPS records (RFC 9460) and WebRTC ICE candidate disclosure.

probes.description: [Manual] DNS rebind: attacker domain returns public IP first, 127.0.0.1 / 169.254.169.254 second. Apps that re-resolve between TOCTOU validation and fetch hit internal target.

[Manual] SVCB / HTTPS resource records (RFC 9460): attacker DNS returns SVCB pointing at internal IP via 'ipv4hint'/'ipv6hint'. HTTP clients that prefer SVCB without re-validating get redirected internally.

k8s_in_cluster_pivot.description: SSRF inside a Kubernetes pod has direct line-of-sight to: kubelet (10250), etcd (2379/2380), kube-apiserver (6443), service-account-token at /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token. Cluster-compromise primitive.

probes.description: kube-apiserver via in-cluster DNS. If pod has default service account with secrets read, attacker dumps cluster secrets.
Service-account JWT token — used to authenticate to kube-apiserver. Combine with API call to escalate.
kubelet pods endpoint — if anonymous-auth enabled (older clusters), lists every pod on the node + container metadata.
etcd v2 API — dumps the entire cluster key/value store including all Kubernetes objects. Modern etcd uses v3 gRPC, but legacy clusters expose v2 HTTP.
